lastMeeting.summary: On 2026-04-24, the Mets lost to the Colorado Rockies 3-4 at Citi Field.
lastMeeting.recapHeadline: Lorenzen's 7 sharp frames a relief for beleaguered Rox staff
recapNotes: Right-hander Michael Lorenzen made his fifth career start against the Mets -- for his fifth different team -- on Friday night at Citi Field.
After facing New York as a member of the Reds, Angels, Tigers and Rangers, Lorenzen was in a Rockies uniform and had his best game of the season as Colorado won, 4-3 .

sections.heading: 8. Game Analysis
sections.body:
Why the Mets have a case
• Nolan McLean gives New York the cleaner run-prevention case in the underlying metrics that are actually available.
• There is at least a plausible positive-regression case if the Mets' quality of contact finally turns into actual runs.
• There is still a workable bullpen path if the game reaches the middle innings in a tie or with a narrow Mets lead.

Where the risk is
• The softer context inputs lean slightly away from New York, and several of those inputs are still incomplete.

Bottom line
The board still leans Mets, but the strongest case is narrow enough that the writeup should stay disciplined.

writeup.pickSummary: The best path starts with Nolan McLean giving New York the steadier underlying pitching line. There is also a reasonable positive-regression case if the Mets' contact quality finally cashes in.
writeup.pickNarrative: At 2-3 over their last 5 games, the Mets are neither riding momentum nor running from a slump. On pure process numbers, Nolan McLean (2.39 FIP) has a 5.22-run edge over Jose Quintana (7.61 FIP) — the kind of gap that shows up in outcomes over time. The supporting case sits in the rotation: New York ranks 6th in MLB by rotation FIP, which means today isn't a random draw — the Mets are throwing from a deep end of the staff. The board has the cleaner shape on the Mets side, and that's the play.

sections.body:
Why the Mets have a case
• Kodai Senga gives New York the cleaner run-prevention case in the underlying metrics that are actually available.
• There is at least a plausible positive-regression case if the Mets' quality of contact finally turns into actual runs.
• There is still a workable bullpen path if the game reaches the middle innings in a tie or with a narrow Mets lead.

Where the risk is
• The softer context inputs lean slightly away from New York, and several of those inputs are still incomplete.

Bottom line
The board still leans Mets, but the strongest case is narrow enough that the writeup should stay disciplined.

writeup.pickSummary: The best path starts with Kodai Senga giving New York the steadier underlying pitching line. There is also a reasonable positive-regression case if the Mets' contact quality finally cashes in.
writeup.pickNarrative: The Mets enter this one at 2-3 over their last 5, a mixed run that makes today more about the matchup than the streak. On pure process numbers, Kodai Senga (5.32 FIP) has a 2.27-run edge over Jose Quintana (7.59 FIP) — the kind of gap that shows up in outcomes over time. Context worth noting: the Mets' rotation ranks 7th in the league by FIP, putting this lineup behind one of the better pitching groups in baseball right now. The board has the cleaner shape on the Mets side, and that's the play.

Bottom line

writeup.pickNarrative: Coming in at 0-5 over the last 5, the Mets haven't found a rhythm lately, but the underlying case here isn't built on results. The pitching numbers actually lean toward Connor Prielipp (0.21 FIP vs Clay Holmes's 4.18), which is the honest read — the Mets case has to come from elsewhere today. Context worth noting: the Mets' rotation ranks 12th in the league by FIP, putting this lineup behind one of the better pitching groups in baseball right now. Taking the Mets here as the best available path, not a strong analytical endorsement — the board leans the other way, but the price and the case hold up.

writeup.pickSummary: The best path starts with Nolan McLean giving New York the steadier underlying pitching line. There is also a reasonable positive-regression case if the Mets' contact quality finally cashes in.
writeup.pickNarrative: New York has been grinding through a tough stretch — 0-5 in the last 5 — which makes the price more interesting than the record suggests. Nolan McLean comes in with a 2.46 FIP against Simeon Woods Richardson's 5.60 — a 3.14-run gap that's the clearest edge on this board today. Context worth noting: the Mets' rotation ranks 12th in the league by FIP, putting this lineup behind one of the better pitching groups in baseball right now. Taking the Mets here. The analytical lean is unambiguous and the matchup structure supports it.

recapNotes: Cubs manager Craig Counsell was asked prior to Saturday’s game about an aspect of his aggressive mid-game decisions involving pinch-hitters.
At times, it has been to replace rookie Moisés Ballesteros, a gifted hitter still developing at the Major League level.

sections.heading: 8. Game Analysis
sections.body:
Why the Mets have a case
• There is at least a plausible positive-regression case if the Mets' quality of contact finally turns into actual runs.
• There is still a workable bullpen path if the game reaches the middle innings in a tie or with a narrow Mets lead.

Where the risk is
• The softer context inputs lean slightly away from New York, and several of those inputs are still incomplete.
• Edward Cabrera owns the better strike-throwing profile, so the mound edge does not sit with New York.

Bottom line
The honest board leans the other way, so the Mets case is more about the clearest plausible path than a full-model endorsement.

lastMeeting.summary: On 2026-04-14, the Mets lost to the Los Angeles Dodgers 1-2 at UNIQLO Field at Dodger Stadium.
lastMeeting.recapHeadline: 'Vintage Yamamoto' as LA ace shakes off leadoff HR, retires 20 straight
recapNotes: The Mets may have struck first, but Yoshinobu Yamamoto was largely in control the rest of the way.
In what went down as a thrilling pitchers' duel between Yamamoto and Mets starter Nolan McLean on Tuesday night, the Dodgers emerged victorious in a 2-1 contest at Dodger Stadium.

sections.heading: 8. Game Analysis
sections.body:
Why the Mets have a case
• There is at least a plausible positive-regression case if the Mets' quality of contact finally turns into actual runs.
• There is still a workable bullpen path if the game reaches the middle innings in a tie or with a narrow Mets lead.

Where the risk is
• Shohei Ohtani owns the better strike-throwing profile, so the mound edge does not sit with New York.
• The softer context inputs lean slightly away from New York, and several of those inputs are still incomplete.

Bottom line
The honest board leans the other way, so the Mets case is more about the clearest plausible path than a full-model endorsement.

Why the Mets have a case
• There is at least a plausible positive-regression case if the Mets' quality of contact finally turns into actual runs.
• David Peterson gives New York the cleaner run-prevention case in the underlying metrics that are actually available.
• There is still a workable bullpen path if the game reaches the middle innings in a tie or with a narrow Mets lead.

Where the risk is
• The softer context inputs lean slightly away from New York, and several of those inputs are still incomplete.

lastMeeting.recapHeadline: A's new home run celebration is amazing, but also somewhat terrifying
recapNotes: A mysterious giant gold chain featuring the head of an elephant sat at the locker of Luis Severino earlier this week, piquing the curiosity of several Athletics players who walked into the visiting clubhouse at Yankee Stadium.
Turns out, Severino ordered the chain and had it delivered on Tuesday for the start of this six-game road trip against the two New York teams.

lastMeeting.recapHeadline: Third time's the charm! Nelson overpowers Mets after pair of rocky outings
recapNotes: As he's grown over the past year as a pitcher, Ryne Nelson has learned to put the results of a start -- good or bad -- behind him quickly and focus on the next one.
But there's little doubt that the right-hander was itching to take the mound Wednesday after his first two starts of the year didn't go the way he wanted.

lastMeeting.recapHeadline: No Soto, no problem: Short-handed Mets erupt again in San Francisco
recapNotes: Maybe the Mets were not just reciting platitudes when they said they had enough talent in the room to overcome Juan Soto’s absence as he dealt with a mild right calf strain .
The Mets have scored 17 runs in 17 frames since Soto went down in the first inning of Friday night’s win against the Giants.

lastMeeting.summary: On 2026-04-03, the Mets beat the San Francisco Giants 10-3 at Oracle Park.
lastMeeting.recapHeadline: Soto to have MRI after early exit in Mets' much-needed outburst
recapNotes: Nobody in the Mets’ sphere ever wanted to hear “ Juan Soto ” and “MRI”’ in the same breath.
Yet that is the reality the team is facing after playing its best all-around game Friday night and swamping the Giants, 10-3 , at Oracle Park to end a three-game losing streak.

officialPick.label: Official Pick: Mets ML
officialPick.explanation: The clearest case for backing the Mets is that the projected lineup still grades better overall, especially in expected offensive quality. That said, this is one of the more self-aware Mets ML spots: the analytical read is not fully on their side, so the brand pick is leaning on the best plausible New York path rather than a clean all-in edge.

lastMeeting.summary: On 2026-04-02, the Mets lost to the San Francisco Giants 2-7 at Oracle Park.
lastMeeting.recapHeadline: Susac's 3 hits in first MLB start spark cheers -- and a little crying
recapNotes: Daniel Susac ’s fiancée Drew Koltko told him heading into Thursday that he’d pick up his first Major League hit in his first at-bat of the game that night.
That’s just the cherry on top of Susac’s ice cream sundae of a ballgame, his first start behind the plate in the Major Leagues.

sections.heading: 8. Game Analysis
sections.body:
Why the Mets have a case
• Nolan McLean gives New York the cleaner run-prevention case in the underlying metrics that are actually available.
• The clearest Mets path is the projected lineup carrying the better overall offensive shape and expected contact quality.
• There is at least a plausible positive-regression case if the Mets' quality of contact finally turns into actual runs.

Where the risk is
• Bullpen support is not a clean Mets edge, especially with both relief groups carrying recent workload.
• The softer context inputs lean slightly away from New York, and several of those inputs are still incomplete.

Bottom line
The board still leans Mets, but the strongest case is narrow enough that the writeup should stay disciplined.

writeup.headline: Mets vs San Francisco Giants: overall lineup quality is the clearest angle
writeup.synopsis: David Peterson vs Robbie Ray sets the matchup, but the strongest supported angle is overall lineup quality. Overall lineup quality only: projected WAR 0.3 vs 0.2, projected lineup wRC+ 91.1 vs 71.9, xwOBA 0.324 vs 0.258. Several key inputs are still missing, so the read should stay conservative.

sections.heading: 1. Matchup Snapshot
sections.body: David Peterson vs Robbie Ray sets the matchup, but the strongest supported angle is overall lineup quality. Overall lineup quality only: projected WAR 0.3 vs 0.2, projected lineup wRC+ 91.1 vs 71.9, xwOBA 0.324 vs 0.258. Several key inputs are still missing, so the read should stay conservative.

sections.heading: 2. Starting Pitching
sections.body: xERA/FIP profile: David Peterson 3.14/3.17 vs Robbie Ray 3.29/4.11; K-BB% 4.0 to 19.0.

sections.heading: 3. Lineup Edge
sections.body: Overall lineup quality only: projected WAR 0.3 vs 0.2, projected lineup wRC+ 91.1 vs 71.9, xwOBA 0.324 vs 0.258.

sections.heading: 4. Bullpen / Context
sections.body: Bullpen shape: xFIP 3.46 vs 3.38, WHIP 1.18 vs 1.21, last 3-day usage 27.2 IP vs 26.0 IP. Series game 1. Rest/travel: Mets 0 days, opponent 0; bullpen tax heavy/heavy.

sections.heading: 5. Why the Mets Have a Case
sections.body: The best Mets case is lineup quality: the projected group carries a small WAR edge and the stronger expected contact profile, even without true handedness-split data.

sections.heading: 6. Where the Risk Is
sections.body: The main concern is that the Mets' contact-quality indicators still have not converted into actual production, so the offense is more projection than payoff right now. Robbie Ray still owns the better K-BB profile, so the strike-throwing edge is on the other side even if the surface numbers are not.

sections.heading: 7. Analytical Lean
sections.body: Opponent. The weighted board leans to the other side, largely because New York is still carrying more real risk than clean support in the current data.

sections.heading: 8. Official MetsMoneyline Pick
sections.body: The clearest case for backing the Mets is that the projected lineup still grades better overall, especially in expected offensive quality. That said, this is one of the more self-aware Mets ML spots: the analytical read is not fully on their side, so the brand pick is leaning on the best plausible New York path rather than a clean all-in edge.

lastMeeting.recapHeadline: With Cards pitcher in a bind, Gold Glover's hose puts out the fire
recapNotes: For the second night in a row at Busch Stadium, a Cardinals starter pitched into the sixth inning but saw his outing screech to a halt before he could record an out in the frame.
Andre Pallante cruised through five innings before the heart of the Mets’ order started to get to him in the sixth.
